perturbation.original_text: Term . The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e for a period of five years after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.changed_text: Term . The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e for a period of one year after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Florida law, particularly for valuable or complex information, potentially failing to provide 'reasonable' protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Trade Secret Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. A one-year term might be deemed unreasonable for certain trade secrets, thereby failing to meet the legal standard for protection under the Act, especially considering the nature of 'Proprietary' information mentioned in the contract.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality term from five years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation states this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Florida law and fails to provide 'reasonable' protection. The Law Explanation further supports this by stating Florida's Trade Secret Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy and a one-year term might be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the nature of 'Proprietary' information. Because the modified contract could be viewed as not maintaining reasonable secrecy, it creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state.
perturbation.changed_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the choice of law to Delaware when the company is based in Florida and the contract is executed there could conflict with Florida's interest in regulating contracts within its jurisdiction, especially if Delaware law offers less protection for trade secrets or confidential information relevant to the agreement, potentially violating public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 687.0304 - Requirements of a Valid Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: This Statute addresses the validity of a choice-of-law provision. By changing the choice of law to Delaware, there may be a violation of the public policy of Florida because all aspects of this contract seem to be more associated with the state of Florida.

F.S. 687.0304 687.0304 Credit agreements. — (1) DEFINITIONS. — For the purposes of this section: (a) “Credit agreement” means an agreement to lend or forbear repayment of money, goods, or things in action, to otherwise extend credit, or to make any other financial accommodation. (b) “Creditor” means a person who extends credit under a credit agreement with a debtor. (c) “Debtor” means a person who obtains credit or seeks a credit agreement with a creditor or who owes money to a creditor. (2) CREDIT AGREEMENTS TO BE IN WRITING. — A debtor may not maintain an action on a credit agreement unless the agreement is in writing, expresses consideration, sets forth the relevant terms and conditions, and is signed by the creditor and the debtor. (3) ACTIONS NOT CONSIDERED AGREEMENTS. — (a) The following actions do not give rise to a claim that a new credit agreement is created, unless the agreement satisfies the requirements of subsection (2): 1. The rendering of financial advice by a creditor to a debtor; 2. The consultation by a creditor with a debtor; or 3. The agreement by a creditor to take certain actions, such as entering into a new credit agreement, forbearing from exercising remedies under prior credit agreements, or extending installments due under prior credit agreements. (b) A credit agreement may not be implied from the relationship, fiduciary, or otherwise, of the creditor and the debtor. History. — s. 1, ch. 89-130.
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the governing law from Florida to Delaware. The Law Explanation says this may violate Florida's public policy. The Perturbation Explanation states that changing the choice of law could conflict with Florida's interest in regulating contracts within its jurisdiction. Because the company is based in Florida, the original contract was executed there, and Delaware law may provide less protection, there is a potential conflict with public policy, thus, a legal contradiction exists. Snippet 1, while not directly addressing choice of law, shows Florida has laws governing commercial relations, implying an interest in governing contracts executed within its borders.

perturbation.original_text: Return of Materials . All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and re main the property of the disclosing party and shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party upon the disclosing party's written request.
perturbation.changed_text: Return of Materials . All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and remain the property of the disclosing party and shall be returned to the disclosing party only if the disclosing party compensates the possessor for the cost of reproduction.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring compensation for the return of confidential information conflicts with the duty to protect trade secrets and proprietary information, potentially violating Florida's Trade Secrets Act by creating a financial barrier to the disclosing party's right to reclaim their property.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 688.003 - Injunctive Relief for Misappropriation of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: This provision discusses injunctive relief for misappropriation. By placing a financial burden on the owner for the return of their trade secrets, it could be argued that the contract is hindering their ability to seek injunctions to protect these secrets. Since injunctions may require prompt action, this financial burden may hinder that.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Texas Administrative Code | State Regulations | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Texas Administrative Code Title 1 - ADMINISTRATION Title 4 - AGRICULTURE Title 7 - BANKING AND SECURITIES Title 10 - COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT Title 13 - CULTURAL RESOURCES Title 16 - ECONOMIC REGULATION Title 19 - EDUCATION Title 22 - EXAMINING BOARDS Title 25 - HEALTH SERVICES Title 26 - HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES Title 28 - INSURANCE Title 30 - ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY Title 31 - NATURAL RESOURCES AND CONSERVATION Title 34 - PUBLIC FINANCE Title 37 - PUBLIC SAFETY AND CORRECTIONS Title 40 - SOCIAL SERVICES AND ASSISTANCE Title 43 - TRANSPORTATION State Regulations Toolbox about
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a requirement for compensation for the return of confidential information, which directly undermines the original obligation for prompt return upon written request. The Perturbation Explanation states that this introduces a barrier for the disclosing party to reclaim their property. The Law Explanation explains that this compensation requirement could hinder the ability to seek injunctions to protect trade secrets because injunctions may require prompt action. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly mention trade secrets, the explanation suggests that the new requirement contradicts the duty to protect such information by creating a financial barrier to its return. Since the promptness requirement is undermined, a contradiction exists.
